title: C / C++ for Reverse Engineering
subtitle: Practical C and C++ reference for reading decompiler output, reconstructing logic, understanding compiler artifacts, and rebuilding routines from assembly or pseudocode.

sections.title: Scope and Use
sections.text: This sheet is focused on understanding C and C++ as they appear in reverse engineering: compiler output, decompiler pseudocode, structs, classes, pointers, arrays, strings, vtables, calling conventions, and common patterns you reconstruct into source. The goal is to help turn assembly or pseudocode into readable models you can reason about, test, and reimplement.

sections.title: Why C / C++ Knowledge Matters in RE
sections.text: Most native binaries you reverse were originally written in C or C++. Even when a decompiler gives you pseudocode, you still need to recognize what the compiler probably started from: loops, switch dispatch, structs, classes, constructors, destructors, string handling, memory allocation, virtual calls, templates, and STL-like containers. Better C/C++ intuition means faster recognition.

sections.title: CORE C TYPES YOU SEE CONSTANTLY

items.value: Pointer to one object of type T
Untyped memory pointer; cast needed for use
Pointer to pointer; common in out-params
Often buffer or string, not always text
Common local array in crackmes and parsers

sections.title: COMMON C LIBRARY FUNCTIONS TO RECOGNIZE

items.value: At least one iteration before test
Compiler or manual control transfer; common in low-level code
Likely fields in the same struct
Strong sign of a real struct layout
Decompiler likely needs a struct type
Struct containing references to other objects
File, packet, or object layout likely recoverable
Hidden first argument in many member functions
Function pointer table at object start is common clue
Zero/init fields, assign vtable, allocate members
Free members, reset state, call delete/free
Can reveal class hierarchy and type names
Calls through vtable or indirect function table
Base/derived object layout with shared prefixes
Many instantiated functions with similar structure
Functions with unusual but patterned names/shapes
Built at runtime to avoid static detection
Can hide raw buffer operations underneath
Lifetime bugs visible in cleanup paths
Track who allocates and who releases
Use calling convention and call sites
How return value is used at callers
Tiny wrappers around imports or real functions
Function ends with jump into another function
Source structure may look distorted in output

sections.title: WHEN TO REBUILD LOGIC IN C OR C++

items.value: Good for struct-heavy file or packet formats
Useful for checksum, validation, crypto-like logic
Helpful when vtables and object state matter
Sometimes easier in C/C++ than Python
Large brute-force or emulation tasks may benefit
Fastest way to verify reversed logic
Debugger integration, patch helpers, file pipelines

Crackmes and Input Generators
sections.text: For authorized crackmes and labs, a common workflow is to translate the target check routine into C, C++, or Python, verify it against the binary, and then solve for valid input or brute force a constrained search space. In practice, this is usually a 'check reimplementation' or 'input generator' rather than anything magical. C/C++ can be useful here when you want the rebuilt model to look very similar to the original native code.

sections.title: EXAMPLE: C CHECK FUNCTION FOR A CRACKME LAB
GOOD HABITS WHEN REBUILDING C / C++ FROM RE

items.value: Use uint8_t/uint32_t/etc. when exact size matters
Note where logic came from: asm, pseudocode, debugger
Especially in bitwise and arithmetic transforms
Rename vars to meaning, not temp1/temp2
Compare rebuilt model against observed runtime behavior
